3A_Bibliographic.citation: Shi, X., Duck, S. A., Jansen, E., Borsarini, B., Blackwell, C. K., Li, Y., & Carnell, S. (2025). Concurrent and prospective associations of social media usage with binge eating symptoms in early adolescence. Obesity (Silver Spring), 33(2), 346-355.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: ABCD 5.1 data release (collected from September 1, 2016, to January 15, 2022)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Baseline demographics and data from the 2-year, 3-year, and 4-year follow-ups (henceforth referred to as Waves 1-3) from the ABCD 5.1 data release (collected from September 1, 2016, to January 15, 2022) were analyzed in the current study. Among 4754 participants included in the ABCD Study 4-year follow-up, we excluded those with missing data on exposure, outcomes, and covariates (with the exception of household income and BMI), resulting in a final total of 1940.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Waves 1-3 in this paper correspond to ABCD 2yr, 3yr, and 4yr follow-ups. Baseline demographics also used.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Social media addiction (SMA) scores and time spent on social media (SMT)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale (adapted, 6-item SMA questionnaire, Cronbach alpha = 0.84) and ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey for SMT
3C_Exposure.operationalization: SMA: categorical (no symptoms score=6; mild symptoms scores 7-23; addiction score>=24). SMT: continuous hours/day (weighted weekday/weekend average)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: From Waves 1 to 3, youth were surveyed about their social media use on weekdays and weekends. The average daily SMT was calculated using the following formula: ([weekday time x 5] + [weekend time x 2])/7. Additionally, youth were asked about their number of social media accounts. Participants with >=1 account were asked to complete a six-item SMA questionnaire (Cronbach alpha = 0.84), adapted from the Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale to assess symptoms of problematic use.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5), self-reports and parent reports
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 12.8% at Wave 1, BE prevalence at Wave 3 not explicitly stated in main text
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Participants' BE symptoms were assessed with both self-reports and parent reports at Waves 1 and 3 using the Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5). Presence of BE symptoms was defined as any episode of losing control and consuming more than needed while eating in the past 2 weeks.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression with robust error variance; latent class trajectory modeling
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, age, race and ethnicity, annual household income, highest parental education, sleep duration, physical activity, screen time, BMI percentile, Wave 1 BE status
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Modified Poisson regression with robust error variance estimated associations of SMT and SMA with BE. For the cross-sectional associations at Wave 1 and Wave 3, prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated. For the prospective association between Wave 1 SMT/SMA and Wave 3 BE, relative risks (RR) with 95% CI were calculated... In addition to the crude model, stepwise adjusted results are presented to increase the specificity of our findings by progressively controlling for potential confounders. Model 1 adjusted for demographic covariates, Model 2 additionally adjusted for BMI percentiles and behavioral covariates, and Model 3, used for the longitudinal analysis, additionally adjusted for Wave 1 BE.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Wave 1 SMT, h - Model 3 RR (95% CI) = 0.99 (0.93-1.06)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Wave 1 SMA mild symptoms (vs. no symptoms)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Mild symptoms - Model 3 RR (95% CI) = 1.37 (1.00-1.87)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Consistently high SMA trajectory (vs. low SMA)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 4: High SMA - Model 3 RR (95% CI) = 3.01 (2.07-4.37)
Table 4: Decreased SMA - Model 3 RR (95% CI) = 1.92 (1.33-2.78)
Table 4: Increased SMA - Model 3 RR (95% CI) = 2.09 (1.59-2.74)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: SMA symptoms, but not time on social media, were cross-sectionally and prospectively associated with BE.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper focuses on relative risk measures. Some RRs are genuinely large (e.g., 3.01 for consistently high SMA). The SMT associations are null. The paper uses p < 0.05 threshold throughout without reporting exact p-values.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Further observational and interventional research could illuminate underlying mechanisms and test the value of targeting social media-addictive behaviors to mitigate the risk of disordered eating.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Managing addictive behaviors rather than focusing solely on SMT may be particularly beneficial, especially for male adolescents, and effects may also differ by body weight.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion distinguishes between time spent on social media (null association) and social media addiction symptoms (significant association with BE). It emphasizes that addictive patterns of use, rather than mere duration, predict binge eating. The paper discusses shared biobehavioral vulnerability as a possible mechanism and appropriately notes limitations including potential for unmeasured confounding and the non-clinical nature of the SMA measure.
